title: Integrators and Robot Adoption: Facts from Hungary
abstract: Industrial robot adoption among firms is uneven and often depends on intermediaries ('integrators') who handle configuration, installation, and maintenance. Using Hungarian firm-level administrative data on robot adoption and buyer-supplier networks, the paper documents a set of empirical facts about integrators: who they are, which firms they serve, and how integrators' capabilities map to their clients' adoption patterns. The facts help explain why robot adoption in Hungary is concentrated in firms with particular supply-chain positions and suggest that the 'integrator' layer is an under-appreciated margin for industrial-policy design.
summary_en: Industrial robots don't spread randomly across firms — the adoption process depends crucially on intermediaries called "integrators" who install, configure, and maintain them. This paper uses Hungarian firm-level administrative data to document empirical facts about the integrator layer: who plays the role, which firms get served, and how integrators' capabilities map to their clients' robot-adoption patterns. The result is a cleaner empirical picture of why robot adoption is concentrated in particular firm segments — and why industrial policy aiming at robotisation should pay attention to the integrator ecosystem, not just to robot-buying firms.
data_used: Hungarian administrative firm-level data on robot purchases and installation services. Buyer-supplier network data linking adopters to integrator firms. Multiple years of recent data.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian industrial-policy design (NGM, Ministry of Economy) and to robot-adoption subsidy programmes (e.g., GINOP / EU cohesion fund schemes). The paper suggests that policy should support integrators as the bottleneck, not only robot-buying firms. Relevant to Ministry of Economy, regional-development agencies, and the evaluation of existing robot-subsidy programmes. Caveat: Hungarian-specific evidence; the exact integrator-ecosystem structure may differ in other EU countries.
